titleStmt.title: Letter from Maria Edgeworth to Frances Anne Edgeworth, November 4, 1818
titleStmt.author: Maria Edgeworth
titleStmt.sponsor: Texas A & M University, University of Tennessee, Wake Forest University, Xavier University of Louisiana, Southern Methodist University
titleStmt.principal: Susan Egenolf, Meredith Hale, Hilary Havens, Jessica Richard, and Robin Runia

editionStmt.edition: First digital edition in TEI, date: February 19, 2026

availability.p: Reproduced by courtesy of Bodleian Library
availability.licence: Distributed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 (CC BY 4.0) Unported License

msDesc.head:
Letter from
Maria Edgeworth 1768-1849 71477273 62567029
to Frances Anne Edgeworth, 1769-1865, , Nov r 4th1818

sourceDesc.bibl: In addition to our edited version, this letter was previously published in

editorialDecl.p: Edgeworth’s spelling and punctuation are retained in transcription, including non-standard spelling, line breaks, and hyphenation of words. The long s and ligatured forms are silently emended.

div.p: The newspapers have told you the dreadful catastrophe - the death and the manner of the death of that great and good man - Sir Samuel Romilly

My dearest mother there seems no end of horrible calamities - - there is no telling [deletion]the[/deletion] how this has been felt in this house - I did not know till now that M[superscript]r[/superscript]. Dugald Stewart had been so very intimate with Sir Samuel and so much attached to him - 40 years his friend! - and now to lose him in this shocking man ner at the moment most dangerous for M[superscript]r[/superscript]. Stewarts own mind - M[superscript]r[/superscript]. S - has been dreadfully shocked - he has not been able to sleep these two nights - he wanders about the house [insertion]walking most feebly, leaning on his daughter[/insertion] a melancholy spectacle - He was just getting better - enjoyed seeing us - con= versed with me quite happily the first evening & [deletion]the[/deletion] I felt reassured [underline]as to his[/underline] [underline]mind[/underline] - But what may be the consequence
of this stroke none can tell - His amicable daughter & M[superscript]rs[/superscript]. Stewart show great re- solution and judgment - I rejoice that we came - they say I am of use conversing with him - The only thing to which he has been able to turn his mind is the M[superscript]s[/superscript] of my fathers life - He says that is very interesting to him - This morning he read [deletion]all[/deletion] the preface and all the history of childhood - His affectionate manner of speaking of him & you at [underline]this time[/underline] is very touching - When he read the account in the papers & Dumonts evi dence and Sir [underline]Samuels letter to Dumont[/underline] he cried bitterly and M[superscript]rs[/superscript]. S- said that relieved him much - Lord Lansdowne, did not sleep Lady L- says and looks wretchedly - He was extreme ly attached to Sir Samuel & besides pri vate friendship as he says the public loss is irreparable - Lord L- can hardly speak on the subject without tears notwithstanding all his efforts - [deletion]Both[/deletion]

The first thought both of the Stewarts and ourselves was to leave this house to leave Lord & Lady L to themselves - But Lord L to whom I spoke directly assured me in the most affectionate manner that
instead of being any a constraint it was a [LibNote]74[/LibNote] comfort to him & to Lady L to have people with him who as he knows feel as they do - How excessively Sir Samuel R was beloved as well as expected - Poor Dumont! - how unjust to think he wanted feeling - His letters to Lord L- during the time of Lady R's illness & all his conduct to them show the tenderest feeling. Nov[superscript]r[/superscript]. 5[superscript]th[/superscript]. This morning Lord L has shewn me a letter [insertion]he has[/insertion] just received from good M[superscript]r[/superscript]. Wish -aw - Sir S on the 4[superscript]th[/superscript]. Oct added a codicil to his will appointing [insertion]M[superscript]r[/superscript]. Waldegrave[/insertion] Lady [deletion]Lans[/deletion] Romilly's brother his executor & guardian to his children - Lady R. had formerly been sole guardian - In case of this gentleman's death or declining to act Sir Samuel appoints M[superscript]r[/superscript]. Wishaw & Lord Lansdowne his executors & guardians to his children - M[superscript]r[/superscript]. Waldegrave [insertion]he changed his name from [underline][unclear]Garbet[/unclear][/underline] for some estate[/insertion], Lady Rs brother [deletion]it is[/deletion] [deletion]tho[/deletion] cannot act he is [insertion]in[/insertion] such bad health - it is thought he will die - It is evident from this codicil that Sir Samuel at that time gave up hope of Lady R - What resolution he shewed in writing it - he has settled all his affairs properly - [deletion]It was feared[/deletion] From his calling for [underline][unclear]that[/unclear][/underline] [underline]sheet of paper[/underline] [deletion]that[/deletion] [insertion]&[/insertion] from his efforts to write it
was feared that he had left no will --
I know my dearest mother how all I this will shock you - I know every thought that will rush on your mind - for the [underline]past[/underline] & for the [underline]future[/underline] ----

M[superscript]rs[/superscript]. D. S [deletion]who[/deletion ] from her knowledge of H- guessed the truth with regard to W from the moment she heard of a brain fever -- she tells me she has had great experience in two different families [deletion]&[/deletion] of intimate friends and has had the advice of the first medical men D[superscript]r.[/superscript] Gregory in particular - He was decidedly of opinion that there was no chance of recovery for a patient unless removed from their friends & their home - He says they compare their actual with their past state at home & this with the worst effect -- But that [underline]elsewhere[/underline] they compare themselves with other patients & have hope [insertion]of cure[/insertion] & motive for exerting themselves to get well & get home again -- In short without [superscript]my[/superscript] attempting to detail the reasons D[superscript]r[/superscript]. G decidedly was of this opinion & in two cases where his opinion was not followed from the injudicious tenderness of friends the consequences were fatal -- once to the patient herself -- and once to & sister - a girl of 15 who only from [underline]seeing[/underline] & [underline]hearing[/underline] what passed with the patient went raging mad - The best chance of preventing any catastrophe [underline]such as we fear from despair[/underline] is M[superscript]rs.[/superscript] Stewart says the trusting to the [underline]watching[/underline] of
persons habituated to such attendance - The patient always finds means to deceive friends [LibNote]75[/LibNote] [deletion]The[/deletion] His having spoken on that subject and having suggested that it might come to that is M[superscript]rs[/superscript]. D. S assures me the best proof that it never will - Those who [underline]say[/underline] never [underline]do[/underline] it - - She has had long experience for many years in several cases -

As I know the course of your mind perfectly my dearest mother I have an- ticipated the painful thoughts which will occur & have offered all that has given me any relief or hope - you know M[superscript]rs[/superscript]. Stewarts affection & discretion there fore you will not have any fears [deletion]from[/deletion] of her speaking on the subject.

Tell Lovell, my dear brother Lovell [deletion]with[/deletion] that M[superscript]r[/superscript]. Stewart remembers him with great affection - Maria Stewart is a charming girl - But I don't mean anything by naming her so immediately after Lovell - My heart is too full of me lancholy things at this moment even look forward to [insertion]the pleasures of[/insertion] [unclear]maneuvering[/unclear] -
The Stewarts stay [insertion]here[/insertion] till Monday - - then they go to D[superscript]r[/superscript]. Millar's Exeter - & look for a house for winter -

We have written to Anna to say that we will go to her on Monday for a few days - I think my dearest
mother[deletion] that[/deletion] after weighing all the reasons pro & con, and after considering all the chances of advantage to Honora and hearing all her wishes and feelings I have come to a decision that it is most prudent as well as certainly most agreeable to us both to return home instead of spending the winter in England -

However I will wait a few days longer before I write to make irrevocable engagements for our way home - I will wait for some letter Conn or packet of yours which has not yet reached me & in which perhaps there may be some reasons or request from you which ought to weigh with us
At all events [deletion]we[/deletion] I will decide not to make any movement towards re- turning till I hear that W. is safe at Tunbridge -

It is my present intention as soon as I hear that he has set out on his journey to write to M[superscript]rs[/superscript]. E Sneyd to offer ourselves to her - My aunts have written to Honora to say that as we refused M[superscript]rs[/superscript]. Sneyds first
invitation it is now my business to[underline] propose[/underline] [LibNote]76[/LibNote] a visit - So I shall - propose staying there a fortnight - [deletion]a week[/deletion] another fortnight between M[superscript]rs[/superscript]. M Powys and the Hol- lands - You can calculate the rest as well as I can -
I believe I must give up going to the Hopes tho it hurts my conscience & my inclinations to so but it w[superscript]d[/superscript] be such an immense round & expense
Adieu some carriage of I know no[damage][unclear]t[/unclear][/damage] what visitors are just come & I must be ready for dinner
I enclose a note from Tom B which shews he is a good [insertion]natured tho silent[/insertion] creature

I beg to have the pleasure of subscribing for that dear little Francis to Library & newspapers - [insertion]as to[/insertion] pocket money I leave the pleasure [underline]justly[/underline] to his dear mother Maria E

postscript.p:
memorandum for future The people that [underline]have been[/underline] here 2 young M[superscript]r[/superscript]. Smiths relations of Lord L's M[superscript]r[/superscript]. & [underline]M[superscript]rs[/superscript]. Ogden[/underline] - he clever - & rich She - the strangest of beauties
Pray get me a piece of bog oak for turning for M[superscript]r[/superscript]. J. Wilson - I have promised it -

Pray write to your [deletion]father[/deletion] brother William & ask him to inquire if a portrait of Spenser can be had at a certain [underline]Castle Saffron[/underline] near Corke - now a ruin - but in wh[superscript]h[/superscript]. there was the only portrait known of Spenser except one in some Scotchman's possession - Does D[superscript]r[/superscript]. B know of any other I promised Lady Spenser to ask -

I have never received your drawings - I have written to Ward - M[superscript]rs[/superscript]. [unclear]J S[/unclear] tells me that Wilkie saw the sketch she has of the Irish dance & was delighted with it - did not know whose it was - said it was [underline]admirable[/underline] -

div.closer: Calne November fifth 1818 M[superscript]rs[/superscript]. Edgeworth Dr Beaufort's [deletion]Edgeworthtown[/deletion] Ireland [underline]Lansdowne[/underline] Near Dunleer
